summary: Jeff Atwood examines guerilla redesigns of famously ugly but successful websites like Craigslist and useit.com, questioning whether such efforts are worthwhile. He argues that for content-based websites, usability improvements are essentially meaningless compared to the value of the content itself. Drawing on Joel Spolsky's observation that applications doing something people really want will succeed despite poor UI, Atwood extends this to content sites. He concludes that 'ghetto' design can be a valid choice when the content or community connection is strong enough, as evidenced by MySpace's success despite its terrible design.
key_points: Redesigning content-heavy sites like Craigslist is largely a waste of time because the content value dwarfs any UI improvements
Applications and websites that do something people really want will succeed despite terrible usability
Content-based websites are already 'so damn usable' that UI consulting adds no measurable value
'Ghetto' design is a valid choice when the platform successfully enables community and communication
The ultimate test of a product is whether it 'gets you laid or gets you paid' - design is secondary to this
money_quotes: An application that does something really great that people really want to do can be pathetically unusable, and it will still be a hit.
There is not a single content-based website online that would gain even one dollar in revenue by improving usability.
The content is such a strong attraction that you could probably change the stylesheet to use green text on a red background and usage would still continue to climb.
The redesign is clearly a small improvement, but it's just a statistical rounding error next to the value of the content.
Get a community together where people can communicate easily and you have yourself a winner.
He'll only fund for two reasons: if it gets you laid, or it gets you paid.
Design is important, but content is king.
key_insight: For content-driven platforms, the value of what users can do or access completely overwhelms any considerations of visual design or usability polish.
